title: Please Excuse My Child: Unexcused Absences in Student Attendance and Achievement
gist: Using statewide Indiana attendance data (2016-2024), the authors show that chronic absenteeism is largely unexcused absenteeism, that unexcused absences are concentrated among the most-absent and disadvantaged students, and that unexcused absences depress test scores more than excused ones, making them the prime target for attendance policy.

themes.theme: Chronic absenteeism is mostly unexcused absenteeism

insights.claim: In Indiana, the spike in absences among the most-absent students is overwhelmingly driven by unexcused absences, while excused absences increase only roughly linearly across absence-rate deciles.
insights.speaker: Nat Malkus
insights.so_what: Policy that targets total absence counts misallocates effort; the lever is the unexcused tail, not the average student.
insights.quote: this spike in absences for students on the right end of the distribution is overwhelmingly driven by unexcused absences
insights.locator: Unexcused Absences Are Uneven Across Students

insights.claim: Students in the 10th absence-rate decile averaged more than twice the unexcused absences of the 9th decile (19 percent vs 7 percent in 2024), and the top decile's unexcused absences alone exceeded the ninth decile's total absences.
insights.speaker: Nat Malkus
insights.so_what: The harm is hyper-concentrated; a tiny share of students drives the bulk of the problem, so targeted interventions can have outsized returns.
insights.quote: students in the 10th absence-rate decile (the 10 percent of students who are most absent) have more than twice as many unexcused absences, on average, as students in the ninth decile
insights.locator: Unexcused Absences Are Uneven Across Students

insights.claim: For chronically absent Indiana students 52 percent of absences were unexcused versus 32 percent for other students, and 55 percent of top-decile absences were unexcused versus 27-33 percent in the bottom eight deciles.
insights.speaker: Nat Malkus
insights.so_what: Confirms chronic absenteeism is structurally distinct in kind, not just degree, from ordinary absence.
insights.locator: A corollary of this spike in unexcused absences

insights.claim: In 2024 about 41 percent of all Indiana absences were unexcused, but only about one-third of the average student's absences were unexcused, because heavy-absence students skew the aggregate.
insights.speaker: Nat Malkus
insights.so_what: Aggregate and per-student metrics diverge sharply, so reporting choice changes the apparent scale of the problem.
insights.quote: about 41 percent of all absences in Indiana were unexcused, but only about one-third of the average student's absences were unexcused
insights.locator: Unexcused Absences Are Uneven Across Students

themes.theme: Documentation vs behavior: the two-explanation framework

insights.claim: The authors frame elevated unexcused absences among disadvantaged students as driven by two non-exclusive mechanisms: a documentation gap (legitimate absences not getting excused) and a behavior gap (genuinely illegitimate absences), arguing both are at work.
insights.speaker: Nat Malkus
insights.so_what: The diagnosis determines the fix: documentation problems call for easier excusal processes, behavior problems call for engagement interventions.
insights.quote: neither of these explanations—differences in classification or differences in behavior—is desirable
insights.locator: Why does this excused-absence gap emerge

insights.claim: Unexcused-absence shares were dramatically higher for disadvantaged groups: 49 percent for poor vs 32 percent for nonpoor students, 67 and 52 percent for black and Hispanic vs 31 percent for white students, and 60 percent in the poorest school quintile vs 23 percent in the least poor.
insights.speaker: Nat Malkus
insights.so_what: Raw demographic gaps in 'unexcused' status are huge and could reflect inequitable recordkeeping, not just behavior.
insights.quote: the unexcused shares of absences were higher among poor than nonpoor students (49 vs. 32 percent)
insights.locator: The first is a difference in documentation

insights.claim: It is implausible that 10 percent of students have unavoidable reasons to miss an average of 34 days (median 30) of school per year, so a substantial share of heavy-absence days are likely choices rather than necessity.
insights.speaker: Nat Malkus
insights.so_what: Supports treating chronic absenteeism partly as an agency/engagement problem, reframing it away from pure circumstance.
insights.quote: it is difficult to believe that 10 percent of students, or even half as many, have unavoidable reasons to miss an average of 34 days of school
insights.locator: The second explanation is differences in behavior

insights.claim: After adding controls, chronically absent Indiana students still had 42 percent of absences unexcused vs 33 percent for others, with odds of an unexcused absence 65 percent higher for chronically absent students and 96 percent higher for the 10th decile, though much smaller than raw gaps.
insights.speaker: Nat Malkus
insights.so_what: Both mechanisms survive controls, so neither a pure-documentation nor pure-behavior story fully explains the pattern.
insights.quote: 42 percent of chronically absent students' absences were unexcused in 2024, compared with 33 percent for non–chronically absent students
insights.locator: Modeling Unexcused Absences Across Student Groups

insights.claim: Controlled odds of an unexcused absence were 89 percent higher for black vs white students, 147 percent higher in the poorest vs least-poor school quintile, 84 percent higher for the top vs lowest achievement quintile, and 50 percent higher in urban vs rural schools.
insights.speaker: Nat Malkus
insights.so_what: Even net of poverty and race, structural correlates of unexcused status persist, complicating simple narratives.
insights.quote: 147 percent higher for the poorest vs. the least poor quintile
insights.locator: Aside from whether a student was in the 10th absence-rate decile

insights.claim: Special-education students had about 15 percent more total absences but, controlling for other factors, 12 percent lower odds that an absence was unexcused, consistent with more documented medical issues.
insights.speaker: Nat Malkus
insights.so_what: Suggests documentation access genuinely shifts excused status, supporting the documentation-gap hypothesis.
insights.locator: Finally, as might be expected with more common medical issues

insights.claim: Over the 2024 Indiana school year unexcused absences rose from 35 percent of all absences in August to 55 percent in May, while excused absences fell through spring, tracking the influenza-like-illness curve.
insights.speaker: Nat Malkus
insights.so_what: The excused/illness coupling and the spring unexcused drift signal that the recordkeeping meaningfully separates sickness from discretionary absence.
insights.quote: unexcused absences rose from 35 percent of all absences in August 2023 to 55 percent of all absences in May 2024

insights.claim: The pandemic changed the quantity but not the quality of absences: the unexcused share in Indiana was only about 4 percent higher in 2023 and 9 percent higher in 2024 versus 2019, and was already drifting up pre-pandemic.
insights.speaker: Nat Malkus
insights.so_what: Counters the framing that post-COVID absenteeism is a new categorical phenomenon; it is the same patterns at higher volume.
insights.quote: the pandemic changed the quantity but not the quality of absences
insights.locator: One theme in recent absenteeism research

insights.claim: An absence was more likely to be unexcused the more prior absences a student had accumulated: the average student's first absence had a 36 percent chance of being unexcused vs 42 percent for their 10th, an ordering effect independent of the student's total absence level.
insights.speaker: Nat Malkus
insights.so_what: Implies escalating disengagement within a year, a window for early-warning intervention before the pattern compounds.
insights.quote: the chance that the average student's first absence was unexcused was 36 percent, compared with a 42 percent chance for their 10th absence
insights.locator: Another pattern that parallels the rise in unexcused absences

insights.claim: Unexcused absences spiked at week's edges far more than excused ones: in 2024 unexcused absences were 36 percent higher on the last school day of the week vs midweek (excused only 13 percent higher), consistent with families extending weekends.
insights.speaker: Nat Malkus
insights.so_what: Friday-spike behavior across all deciles points to discretionary choice rather than illness, sharpening the target for nudges.
insights.quote: students and families more often choose to take off the week's last school day to extend their weekends
insights.locator: Finally, we look at excused- and unexcused-absence patterns

insights.claim: Unexcused absences were associated with larger test-score declines than excused absences in both math and ELA; for grades four to eight in 2024 unexcused absences were 38 percent of absences but accounted for 49 percent of math and 67 percent of ELA absence-related score declines.
insights.speaker: Nat Malkus
insights.so_what: Disproportionate learning loss from unexcused days is the empirical case for prioritizing them in interventions.
insights.quote: Unexcused absences were associated with 49 percent of the total absence-related test-score declines in math and 67 percent of the declines in ELA
insights.locator: Excused and Unexcused Absences and Test Scores

insights.claim: The authors caution that reducing unexcused absences may not improve scores by the observed amounts because student engagement drives both, and warn the goal is converting unexcused absences into attendance, not into excused absences.
insights.speaker: Nat Malkus
insights.so_what: Flags a causality caveat against naive policy that just reclassifies absences to hit chronic-absenteeism targets.
insights.quote: the appropriate goal is to convert unexcused absences into attendance
